title: 6.2 and 6.3 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lessons 6.1-6.3!
topic_path: The First Global Age (1200 to 1750 CE)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What factors allowed the Mongols, a small nomadic tribe, to build the largest empire in world history?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: skilled military tactics, strong leadership, and adaptability
superior naval power and control of major sea routes
advanced written laws and early industrialization
isolation from other empires and focus on internal development

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes how the Mongols dominated trade across their empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They secured trade routes, protected merchants, and standardized taxes.
They built a navy to control maritime trade routes.
They isolated regions to protect local economies.
They limited trade to goods produced within Mongol territory.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best explains the importance of trade to the Mongol Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade was promoted and protected, helping to link distant parts of the empire.
Trade was discouraged in order to keep the empire self-sufficient.
Trade was heavily taxed, which led to resistance and rebellions.
Trade was limited to Mongol merchants and forbidden to outsiders.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes the lasting impact of the Mongol Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It increased contact between cultures through trade and exchange.
It established lasting Mongol rule throughout Europe.
It caused trade routes between regions to break down.
It ended the use of horses in warfare across Asia.

assessment_items.question_content:
>He [Kublai Khan] caused the roads to be cleaned and kept in order, and set up stations with horses and men every twenty-five miles… In this way, merchants and travelers could pass safely through his empire, no matter how far they traveled.  

>—Marco Polo, The Travels of Marco Polo, late 13th century


**What does this passage suggest about the Mongol Empire?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Mongols created systems to support safe travel and communication.
Travel across the empire was dangerous and discouraged.
The empire lacked infrastructure to support long-distance trade.
Only Mongol citizens were allowed to travel freely.

assessment_items.question_content:
>The Tartars [Mongols] are a savage race, thirsting after blood. They spare neither age nor sex… Wherever they pass, they leave ruin and desolation in their wake.  

>—Matthew Paris, English monk and chronicler, c. 1240


**What does this passage reveal about how some perceived the Mongols?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They were viewed as cruel and destructive invaders.
They were admired for their military skills and tactics.
They believed the Mongols brought peace and unity to Europe.
They envied their wealth and success.

assessment_items.question_content:
Why is the Silk Road exchange network important when studying the spread of the bubonic plague in the fourteenth century?

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People, rats, and fleas moving along the Silk Road helped the plague spread farther and faster.
Misinformation spread rapidly on these routes, making medical responses unreliable.
Knowledge spread along the Silk Road, building learning that led to a cure.
It collapsed entirely due to the plague, ending trade between East and West.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What role did fourteenth-century trade networks play in the transmission of the Black Death?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They facilitated the rapid spread of the plague by connecting distant populations.
They had little impact, as the disease spread primarily through local contact.
They helped contain the disease by isolating infected regions.
They were used to deliver medical aid and slow the spread of the disease.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what way can the period of peace known as the Pax Mongolia be linked to the spread of the Black Death?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Mongol Empire expanded and protected trade routes, helping the plague spread widely.
The Catholic Church launched peaceful Crusades that increased travel and disease.
Enlightenment thinkers promoted physical contact, spreading the disease.
The Abbasid Caliphate increased camels to carry goods, but camels spread plague-carrying fleas.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What positive outcomes emerged in European society after the devastation of the plague?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increased wages and improved living conditions for many workers
A significant decline in agricultural production due to the loss of labor
A decrease in warfare, as there had been enough death and destruction
Advancements in science that ensured another epidemic would not occur

assessment_items.question_content:
>It was falsely reported that the Jews had poisoned the water wells in many towns and villages, causing the plague to spread. As a result, the Jewish communities were attacked and many were killed.  

>—Account of the Strasbourg Massacre, 1349


**What does this passage reveal about the treatment of Jewish communities during the Black Death?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Jewish communities were unfairly blamed for the plague and violently persecuted.
Jews were welcomed into new cities for their medical expertise.
The government protected Jews from false accusations during the plague.
Jewish religious leaders were asked to help lead efforts to stop the disease.

assessment_items.question_content:
>Ships brought the plague to Messina. Those who saw them coming found that most of the sailors were already dead. The port was closed, and trade was halted, but the sickness had already spread.  

>—Gabriele de’ Mussis, Chronicle of the Plague in Italy, c. 1348


**What does Gabriele de’ Mussis’s account suggest about the relationship between trade and the spread of the Black Death?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Trade routes, especially by sea, helped the plague reach Europe quickly.
Trade completely stopped before the plague entered Europe.
Trade mainly affected the economy rather than the spread of disease.
European ports refused to take action when sick sailors arrived.
